mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X6 M50i or M60i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned N63 or S68 4.4-litre V8 engine typically costs £6,000–£12,000 supply only. Used engines are available from £4,500–£7,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on your location and workshop. Labour times can exceed 30 hours for a full V8 replacement. Main dealer new engines can exceed £15,000.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X6 M50i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N63TU3 (M50i) is the most refined version of BMW's original twin-turbo V8. Early N63s (2008–2013) had a well-documented reputation for excessive oil consumption, valve stem seal failure, timing chain stretch, and failing injectors. The N63TU3 addressed many of these issues, but the engine still demands meticulous maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X6 M60i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S68 is BMW's latest V8, introduced in 2022. It's too new to have a long-term reliability track record. Early reports indicate potential issues with oil leaks, fuel injector failures, and overheating. The engine is complex and requires regular maintenance. It's promising but unproven long-term.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X6 M50i and M60i?

acceptedAnswer.text: M50i (N63TU3): Excessive oil consumption (valve stem seal failure), timing chain guide wear, oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and oil filter housing, turbocharger issues, and cooling system failures. M60i (S68): Oil leaks, fuel injector failures, overheating, and electrical issues. The specific issues depend on which engine you have.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X6 V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and which generation. Against a well-kept £40,000 M50i, an £8,000 recon is 20% — worth it. Against a tired £20,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. M60i values are higher — a £9,000 recon against a £60,000 car is 15% — definitely worth it.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. G06 M50i (2019–2023): N63B44T3 / N63TU3. G06 M60i (2023–present): S68B44A. The chassis code and badge are the easiest tells — M50i for 2019–2023, M60i for 2023–present.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW X6 M50i / M60i Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the N63 and S68 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 engines. Used N63 engines typically sit in the £4,500–£7,500 band; reconditioned units range from £6,000–£10,000. The S68 is newer and rarer, commanding higher prices. Labour times for a full V8 replacement can exceed 30 hours.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X6 V8 engine typically takes 18–25 hours at £80–£120/hr (independent) or £140–£200/hr (London/SE dealer). The hot-vee V8 design and tight engine bay make this a labour-intensive job.

cta.label: → Compare X6 V8 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Excessive Oil Consumption — N63 (M50i)
problems.symptoms: Low oil level warning light, need to add 1 quart of oil every 600–1,000 miles, blue smoke from exhaust on startup, visible oil consumption between services.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£4,000 (valve stem seal replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £6,000–£12,000 (if oil starvation leads to engine failure)
problems.recommendation: The N63's most notorious issue is excessive oil consumption. Class action lawsuits have been filed alleging owners had to add 6–8 quarts of oil between services. The primary cause is valve stem seal failure, allowing oil to enter the combustion chamber. If you notice blue smoke on startup or are constantly topping up oil, have the valve stem seals inspected. Ignoring this can lead to catalytic converter failure and, in extreme cases, engine damage from oil starvation.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil consumption guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Guide Wear — N63 (M50i)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, reduced performance.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,500 (timing chain guide replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £6,000–£12,000 (if chain skips and valves contact pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The N63's timing chain guides are a known weak point. Plastic guides can become brittle and break over time, sending metal fragments through the engine. The N63TU3 (M50i) has improved guides, but the issue hasn't been eliminated. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain and guides inspected immediately. Regular oil changes using BMW LL-01 approved oil are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks & Cooling System Issues — N63 & S68 (M50i & M60i)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from the engine bay, low oil level warning, visible oil residue around the valve covers or oil filter housing area, coolant loss.
problems.recommendation: Oil leaks from the valve cover gaskets and oil filter housing are common on the N63. The hot-vee design means the engine runs hot, which accelerates gasket degradation. The S68 has also been reported to suffer from oil leaks, with some owners experiencing issues within the first 50,000 miles. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the engine inspected promptly. Left unchecked, oil loss can lead to low oil levels and potential engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: X6 M50i / M60i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Specialist repair; far cheaper than engine replacement
Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Specialist repair; cheaper than engine replacement
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X6 V8? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented valve stem seal replacement history (N63)
* Timing chain guides inspected or replaced (N63)
* Oil consumption within acceptable limits (not requiring constant top-ups)
* Full service history with BMW LL-01 approved oil
* Post-2023 S68 preferred for the latest technology (though long-term reliability is unproven)
avoidIf: * Blue smoke on startup (valve stem seals — N63)
* Excessive oil consumption with no documented repair (N63)
* Poor service history — these V8s are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X6 M50i and M60i represent the pinnacle of BMW's V8 SUV engineering. The M50i's N63TU3 is the most refined version of BMW's original twin-turbo V8 — significantly improved over early iterations but still demanding meticulous maintenance. The M60i's S68 is BMW's latest V8, featuring a 48V mild-hybrid system and representing a technological leap — but it's too new to have a long-term reliability track record. Both are powerful, luxurious, and rewarding machines. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained X6 V8 is a £40,000–£80,000 performance SUV; a neglected one is a £10,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare X6 V8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X6 M50i or M60i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking (timing chain guide warning — N63)
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke on startup indicates valve stem seal failure (N63)
* Oil Consumption — How often does it need topping up? (1 quart per 600–1,000 miles is typical but excessive — N63)
* Service History — including which engine (N63TU3 or S68) and confirmed oil-change intervals using BMW LL-01 approved oil
* Oil Leak Check — Any evidence of oil leaks around the valve covers or oil filter housing?
* Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss or overheating?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, oil, or fuel system issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
